title: Dog Rapid Shallow Breathing at Rest: Is It an Emergency?
meta_desc: If your dog has rapid shallow breathing at rest, it may be a high-priority emergency. Learn why tachypnea occurs and when to seek urgent vet care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Rapid shallow breathing (over 30 breaths per minute at rest) is a life-threatening veterinary emergency that requires immediate medical evaluation to address potential heart or lung failure.

What does it mean if my dog is breathing rapidly and shallowly?

Rapid, shallow breathing—medically referred to as tachypnea—occurs when a dog takes more than 30 breaths per minute while in a relaxed or sleeping state. Unlike normal panting used for cooling down after exercise, this breathing pattern involves quick, short breaths that don't allow the lungs to fill completely. It is often a sign that the body is struggling to maintain proper oxygen levels or is compensating for an underlying physical distress.

Is rapid shallow breathing in my dog a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. Rapid shallow breathing at rest is a critical veterinary emergency that requires immediate medical evaluation.
  • This symptom is often linked to serious conditions such as congestive heart failure, pulmonary edema (fluid in the lungs), pneumonia, or internal injury.
  • Seek emergency care without delay if your dog’s gums appear blue, purple, or pale, or if they are stretching their neck out to breathe.

How can a photo or video help the vet triage my dog’s breathing?

  • Providing a short video allows the triage team to visually assess the respiratory effort and count the breaths per minute.
  • A video helps staff check for 'abdominal breathing,' where the stomach muscles work hard to move air.
  • This visual evidence helps the veterinary staff determine the severity of the situation and prepare oxygen therapy or stabilization equipment before you arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

An increased sleeping respiratory rate (SRR) greater than 30 breaths/min in a dog at rest is abnormally high and indicates tachypnea. This finding, along with increased respiratory effort, can indicate respiratory disease or failure. Dogs exhibiting rapid, shallow breathing at rest may require immediate veterinary attention, and if hypoxemia is unresponsive to oxygen supplementation (PaO2 <60 mmHg or SpO2 <90%), or hypercarbia is present (PaCO2 >60 mmHg), manual or mechanical ventilation may be necessary.

Chapter: Cardiology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 102)

faqs.question: What is a normal resting respiratory rate for a dog?
faqs.answer: A healthy dog typically takes between 15 and 30 breaths per minute while resting or sleeping.

faqs.question: How do I count my dog's respiratory rate?
faqs.answer: Watch the chest rise and fall; one rise and fall equals one breath. Count how many breaths occur in 30 seconds and multiply by two.

faqs.question: Can anxiety cause rapid shallow breathing?
faqs.answer: While anxiety can cause panting, rapid breathing that persists when the dog is supposedly 'at rest' or sleeping is more likely a physical health issue than a behavioral one.
